card.title: Why Sugar Makes You Crash
card.teaser: Why does a donut feel amazing for 20 minutes — and then turn you into a zombie? Meet the hormonal rollercoaster.
card.coverImagePrompt: A glossy pink-frosted donut casting a long jagged shadow that looks like a crashing stock chart, moody editorial food photography, dramatic side lighting

lessons.title: The 3 PM zombie
lessons.body: You grab a muffin and a latte at 2:30. Twenty minutes later, you feel on top of the world. By 3:30, you're staring at your keyboard, your brain replaced with warm pudding. What actually happened inside your body between those two moments? A very predictable hormonal ambush.

visual.imagePrompt: An exhausted office worker slumped at a desk at 3pm, empty pastry bag and coffee cup, golden afternoon light, cinematic

lessons.title: Meet blood glucose
lessons.body: Every carb you eat — bread, fruit, soda, even pasta — eventually becomes glucose, a simple sugar that travels in your blood to fuel your cells. Your body is obsessive about keeping blood glucose in a narrow range. Too low and your brain stalls. Too high and it damages tissues. The dial that keeps this balance is a hormone called insulin, made in the pancreas.

keyTerms.definition: The simple sugar that fuels nearly every cell in your body
The hormone that tells cells to absorb glucose from the blood

visual.imagePrompt: A microscopic view of red blood cells flowing past glowing glucose molecules, medical illustration, cool blue tones

lessons.title: The spike and the overshoot
lessons.body: A donut floods your bloodstream with glucose in minutes. Your pancreas panics and releases a big wave of insulin to clear it. But the body often overshoots — dumping in more insulin than strictly needed. Glucose drops fast, sometimes dipping below your pre-donut baseline. Your brain, which runs almost entirely on glucose, reads this as an emergency. That's the crash: foggy thinking, irritability, and a craving for more sugar.

visual.imagePrompt: A line graph showing blood glucose spiking sharply then crashing below baseline, elegant infographic, warm accent colors

lessons.title: Fiber and fat are brakes
lessons.body: Continuous glucose monitor studies show the same number of carbs produces wildly different spikes depending on what's with them. A slice of bread alone spikes blood sugar hard. The same bread with butter and avocado spikes it much less — fat and fiber slow digestion, so glucose trickles in instead of flooding. Even the order you eat food matters: eating vegetables before rice can cut the spike by roughly a third.
lessons.funFact: A ripe banana spikes blood sugar more than a scoop of vanilla ice cream. The ice cream's fat dramatically slows sugar absorption.

visual.imagePrompt: A bowl of salad next to a bowl of rice with a small clock in the corner showing 'eat this first', clean lifestyle photography

lessons.title: Smooth, not spiky
lessons.body: Your afternoon energy isn't just willpower — it's chemistry. Eating carbs alongside protein, fat, or fiber transforms a jagged glucose spike into a gentle hill. You'll feel less hungry, think more clearly, and skip the 3 PM zombie entirely. You don't have to fear sugar. You just have to stop sending it in alone.

quiz.question: What hormone clears glucose from the blood?
quiz.explanation: Insulin, released by the pancreas, signals cells to absorb glucose from the bloodstream.

quiz.question: Why does a 'sugar crash' leave you feeling foggy?
options: Insulin overshoots and drives glucose below baseline, starving your brain
quiz.explanation: After a big sugar spike, the insulin response often overshoots, briefly dropping blood glucose below normal and leaving your brain underfueled.

quiz.question: Which pairing causes the smallest glucose spike?
options: White rice with vegetables and protein
A ripe banana on its own
quiz.explanation: Fat, fiber, and protein slow digestion, so the same carbs cause a gentler, longer glucose rise when eaten together.

sources.title: Personalized Nutrition by Prediction of Glycemic Responses
